cards.back: A special type of pointer that can point to objects of any data type (generic pointer)

cards.text: To declare a void pointer: {{c1::void* ptr{};}}

cards.question: Is this code valid?
cards.answer: Yes, this is valid. A void pointer can point to any type of object, so it can hold the address of an int or a float.
cards.explanation: Void pointers are generic and can be assigned addresses of any data type without casting.

cards.front: Can you directly dereference a void pointer?
cards.back: No. The compiler doesn't know what type of object the void pointer points to, so it doesn't know how to dereference it. You must first cast to a typed pointer.

cards.text:
To dereference a void pointer, first cast it to the correct type:
void* ptr{ &value };
int* intPtr{ {{c1::static_cast<int*>(ptr)}} };
std::cout << {{c2::*intPtr}};

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot dereference a void pointer directly. Must cast to int* first: std::cout << *static_cast<int*>(ptr);
cards.explanation: Void pointers don't have a type, so the compiler doesn't know how to interpret the data. Must cast to the correct pointer type.

cards.front: Can you do pointer arithmetic with void pointers?
cards.back: No. Void pointers don't have a type, so the compiler doesn't know the size of the object being pointed to, which is required for pointer arithmetic.

cards.explanation:
Void pointers cannot:
  • Be dereferenced directly (must cast first)
  • Perform pointer arithmetic (no type size information)
  • Know what they're pointing to (type information lost)
They can only:
  • Hold an address
  • Be compared with other pointers
  • Be cast to another pointer type

cards.front: When are void pointers typically used in C++?
cards.back:
• C-style functions that work with multiple types (e.g., memcpy, qsort)
  • Interfacing with C libraries
  • Low-level memory operations
In modern C++, prefer templates or std::any instead of void pointers.

cards.text: The C standard library function {{c1::qsort}} uses void pointers to sort arrays of any type: void qsort({{c2::void* base}}, size_t num, size_t size, int (*compare)(const void*, const void*));

cards.concept: Why prefer templates over void pointers in modern C++
cards.explanation:
Templates are type-safe and checked at compile time:
  • Compiler catches type errors
  • No casting needed
  • No runtime overhead
  • Better performance
Void pointers:
  • No type safety - cast errors cause undefined behavior
  • Require manual casting
  • Can't dereference or do arithmetic

cards.question: What's dangerous about this code?
cards.answer: The void pointer originally pointed to a float, but is being cast to int*. Dereferencing as int interprets the float's bytes as an int, giving a garbage value.
cards.explanation: Void pointers lose type information. You must remember and cast back to the original type, or you get undefined behavior.

cards.front: What happens if you delete a void pointer that points to dynamically allocated memory?
cards.back: It causes undefined behavior. The compiler doesn't know the type, so it can't call the destructor. You must cast to the correct type before deleting.

cards.text: Void pointers cannot be {{c1::dereferenced}} or used for {{c2::pointer arithmetic}} because they lack {{c3::type information}}.
To use a void pointer, you must {{c1::cast}} it back to the {{c2::original type}} before dereferencing: static_cast<{{c3::int*}}>(voidPtr)
In modern C++, prefer {{c1::templates}} or {{c2::std::any}} over void pointers for {{c3::type safety}} and compile-time checking.
